author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
While energy density is a critical parameter of a battery, it would be helpful to see the other important battery parameters, such as cell EMF, internal resistance and by implication voltage regulation, self discharge rate, operating temperature range, number of recharge cycles before failure.
It could be argued that measurement of energy density can be used as a filter to select the most promising battery chemistry for further research, but sooner or later the other parameters must be characterized in order to establish where the particular battery chemistry might find application. Once the target application has been identified, then the chemistry can be developed to optimise the performance for this application. I suspect that many who view these videos would very much like to know about the other battery parameters that do not get mentioned. The energy density figures of  your batteries are very impressive, but the other parameters, mentioned above, would indicate potential applications. For example, the electric bicycle battery looked very promising and I am very surprised that you have not taken it into production. Without further information, it must be assumed that while it has
demonstrated an impressive energy density, one of its other parameters has made it unsuitable for commercial manufacture in this application. The question is which parameter: self discharge rate, number of recharge cycles before failure, mass production manufacturability? 
The videos have shown the strong points of the battery chemistries you are researching, namely energy density, but what are their weaknesses?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: understood mate - but these other parameters are talked about in the other videos

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith I agree you have talked about these other parameters, but I am finding it difficult to locate the measurement figures and even harder to associate them with a particular battery. You have every right to say that is not your concern or that this information is company confidential. I am extremely impressed by your electric bicycle battery. It appears to have many advantages over alternative technologies. It is light, low cost, contains no toxic chemicals and has a high energy density, just what industry is search for. So which of its other critical parameters has stopped it being adopted for mass production. It cannot be its internal resistance, since it drives a low impedance electric motor. It must be something to do with reliability or manufacturability.
Maybe it can only be recharged a limited number of times or has a very high self discharge rate or the electrolyte evaporates. I find it difficult to understand why battery manufactures are not banging on your door, trying to gain access to this technology.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't really talk about who is and is not knocking at the door mate

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith I apologise if I have overstepped the mark, and I fully understand commercial confidentiality.
I am looking forward to being able to use graphite based batteries and super caps in my designs.
When comparing oranges, it is not only the colour and size that is important, it is how sweet they are and how difficult to peel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not a worry mate - but i just can't talk about everything - i try to be as open as i can but there are limits - we will be covering some of the stuff later but sometimes things just have to wait and somethings can never be talked about - i know you understand

author: @wyattconroy1541
text:
Your "apple" is well suited toward flat cells common in phones, tablets and even cars.  Amazingly enough, many rectangular car batteries end up making rectangles out of cylindrical batteries rather than just making rectangular batteries from rectangular cells.  Since the voltages are typically relatively high (72V+), you could just stack several rectangles of your carbon foam.  If thickness spec is too great for the foam thickness you can just stack 2+ sets and alternate -,+,-,+,... so for 72V (assuming 2V/cell) it could be [-] 36 layers [+] 36layers [-] ... I put the negative terminals (current collectors) on the outside because we use negative as ground here in the states, but since the design is bidirectional, it wouldn't matter.  I'd hate to see you wasting time and money on making cylindrical batteries that will just be made back into inefficient (space-wise) rectangles.  I'm sure any of the electric car (maybe phone/tablet) manufactures you contact would consider funding a trial run (Tesla would be a good bet)  Maybe do a kickstarter/gofundme/etc for rolls of the stuff (and perhaps rolls pre-backed with current collectors) and see what the community can make.
You could even integrate the current collector with the foam itself using https://www.google.com/patents/US5932185
In case you ever want amorphous carbon: https://www.hindawi.com/journals/jchem/2016/7840687/
BTW another use of your graphetized carbon would be ceramic filters (especially if copper chloride is used in the graphetization process due to its anti-microbial properties) because what good is cheap power storage if you can't get clean drinking water.

replies.author: @wyattconroy1541
replies.text: It may be easier for you to hook up with Dyson locally.  Apparently they have invested 2.5 billion pounds in technology for a new electric car.  Lets hope it doesn't suck or blow like the rest of their products.

author: @JamesDoylesGarage
text: Dear Mr. Murry-Smith. A lot of us love you for the sharing human being you are. Sincerely. And I know there's a part of you that feels that you can't stand the bother of fussing with video\audio technology, and that we should just "Get over it " and focus of the wealth of knowledge you deliver. As an adult, I can respect and value this insincerity. But here is a major problem I hope you can value of mine. My son is 11. He wants to understand you. He's Canadian and is already fighting a little to understand through that wonderful accent of your. Please Please Please let him hear and enjoy the wonderful knowledge you wish to share. Get a mic please? Any mic. Even a boom mic. But a wired Lav mic is cheap and easy. I'm sorry if you are mad I've asked. It's just that what you say is of great value to us all. Especially the next generation of scientist :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate - I use a zoom h2n voice recorder - I did for a while try a lapel mic - I think the real problem is that the videos are made in the lab not a recording studio. To be honest the real answer is to do the video in the lab and the audio in a recording studio and dub the audio over the video via an editor. Now comes the real problem - when make a 10 minute video it takes - all told - 2 hours - from shooting to finished video - most of my videos are 20 minutes long - some are longer. I get around 200 emails a day and I also try to answer them - that takes two hours a day - then I have my own work to do - there is just not enough time in a day. Already the videos are becoming too onerous and I am considering stopping to do them. So I am afraid it is unlikely I will spend more time on them in a recording and editing studio - it is much more likely I will just stop doing them

replies.author: @JamesDoylesGarage
replies.text: We understand. Wish a corporation sponsored you. You could get an intern. lol. We still love you. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thinking about it mate

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text: Power output may not be the best figure of merit when comparing say a nuclear bomb with a nuclear power station. As you say it is very application sensitive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith it is my understanding that research differs from development in the following ways.

Research: the fully characterization and understanding of an observed phenomena.
Development: the design of a product to meet or exceed the product parameters defined by a written Requirement Specification.

As an aside do you any views on the new solid glass electrolyte sodium battery being developed by the team led by John Goodenough. As I understand it, this battery works in a new way not described by classical electrochemistry.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that may well be the defenition of the difference between pure and applied research mate - i love what goodenough is doing - i am not sure what you mean by 'classical' electrochemistry

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text: I have not been able to find any papers on the new battery that I can access as I am not a member of the RSC. The closest I can get is the following link:  http://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2017/EE/C6EE02888H#!divAbstract . I did find a YouTube video from a respected source that explains what I mean by its operation not being explained by classical electrochemistry. The problem appears to be that during charging both electrodes become coated in the same metal, which should ensure zero potential difference between them. I suppose I should wait until the Goodenough team publish details of how they think the new battery works in a paper I can access.  The above abstract talks about Fermi levels in the same fashion as in a silicon diode, maybe there is a similarity between the bias voltage across the diode and the new battery EMF.This is the link https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YAg_8iCLIIw.
